text: The patient is positioned supine with legs open, and pneumoperitoneum is established using a varus needle in the left hypochondrium. The operating table is in Trendelenburg position with the patient's right side tilted down. Four trochars are inserted. The surgeon identifies the intestinal malrotation, with the entire colon positioned on the left and the small bowel on the right. The initial surgical step involves dissecting adhesions between the left and right colon.
The surgeon opens the mesosigma and mobilizes the sigmoid colon. The sigmoid and upper rectal arteries are identified and divided using ligature.

The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon by opening the peritocolic groove, avoiding mobilization of the splenic flexure. Dissection continues down to the recto-sigma junction, where the sigmoid colon is divided using a linear stapler.

The surgeon removes the distal part of the specimen from the abdominal cavity through an incision. Fluorescence angiography reveals a non-vascularized colon segment, prompting a change in the section line. The colon is transducted along the newly marked line, following the division of the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. Dissection continues up to the splenic angle to prevent tension, with subsequent fluorescence angiography confirming complete vascularization of the remaining colon.

The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the resected ends of the colon. A fluorescence angiography is repeated to confirm adequate vascularization of the anastomosed segment.
